cot_content:
Okay, let's think about how to tell right and left ear buds apart without looking. The question gives two options: tying a knot at the connecting end or the speaker end of the left earbud.

First, I need to understand which end is which. The "connecting end" would be the part of the earbud that connects to the device or the wire (if they are wired earbuds), while the "speaker end" is the part that goes into the ear and produces sound.

If I tie a knot at the connecting end (option A), it might be harder to tell right from left because both earbuds have connecting ends. The knot might interfere with the connection or make it harder to plug in. Also, if you're trying to identify which earbud is which by touch, a knot at the connecting end might not be easily distinguishable when you're holding the earbud.

If I tie a knot at the speaker end (option B), it would create a small bump or texture that you can feel when you put the earbud in your ear. This would make it easier to identify left and right by touch. For example, the left earbud with a knot would go into the left ear, and the right one without a knot would go into the right ear. The knot serves as a tactile marker to distinguish them without looking.

So, option B seems more practical for identifying right and left earbuds by touch, as the knot at the speaker end would be easily noticeable when you insert the earbud into your ear.
label: majority
